title: Generator Expressions
beginnerExplanation: A Generator Expression is like a shortcut to write a generator. It looks exactly like a list comprehension, but uses parentheses () instead of brackets []. It produces values one at a time, keeping your computer memory clean.
intermediateExplanation: Unlike list comprehensions which evaluate eagerly and create a full list in RAM, generator expressions evaluate lazily, returning a generator iterator object immediately.
advancedExplanation: Generator expressions compile to the same bytecode as a generator function with a single yield loop. They can be passed directly as arguments to functions like sum(), min(), or max() without inner parentheses.

realWorldAnalogy.title: The Bakery vs. The Recipe
realWorldAnalogy.description: A list comprehension is like buying 100 donuts and storing them in your fridge. A generator expression is like having a recipe for 100 donuts—you only make a donut when someone asks to eat one.

mentalModel: Parentheses mean 'prepare the stream', while brackets mean 'create the list now'.
visualDiagram:
List Comp: [x for x in range] -> allocates full array in memory
Gen Exp: (x for x in range) -> returns generator object with pointer
executionWalkthrough: 1. The expression (x * 2 for x in range(3)) is compiled.
2. A generator object is created instantly without computing products.
3. Elements are calculated only as next() is called or during iteration.

memoryState.note: Only allocates 104 bytes regardless of size

commonMistakes: Thinking a generator expression returns a tuple (use tuple() explicitly if you need a tuple!).
Consuming the expression once and trying to iterate over it again (it will be empty!).

debuggingMission.scenario: A student gets an empty sum after trying to reuse a generator expression.
debuggingMission.brokenCode:
evens = (x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0)
print(sum(evens))  # 20
print(list(evens)) # [] - Empty!
debuggingMission.solution:
evens_func = lambda: (x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0)
print(sum(evens_func()))
print(list(evens_func()))
debuggingMission.explanation: Generator expressions are exhaustible. Re-generate the expression or convert it to a list if you need multiple passes.

practiceQuestions.question: Which delimiters define a Generator Expression?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Generator expressions are defined using round parentheses () instead of square brackets [].

challengeQuestions.question: How do you calculate the exact size in bytes of a generator expression in memory?
hints: Import sys and use its size method.

projectExercise.title: Evaluate Expression Footprint
projectExercise.description: Compare list comprehension size against generator size.
requirements: Use sys.getsizeof on list and generator.
projectExercise.starterCode:
import sys
list_comp = [x for x in range(1000)]
gen_exp = (x for x in range(1000))
print(sys.getsizeof(list_comp) > sys.getsizeof(gen_exp))

bossBattle.name: Sentinel of the Stream
bossBattle.description: Feed a generator expression to the Sentinel to satisfy its memory restrictions.
bossBattle.mechanic: Pass generator expressions inside math filters.

complexityAnalysis.timeComplexity: O(1) initialization. O(N) total traversal time.
complexityAnalysis.description: Saves massive memory when filtering or reducing large collections.

memoryImpact.optimizationNotes: Excellent for reducing memory pressure in microservices processing massive rows.

performanceNotes: Slightly faster startup time than list comprehensions, but slightly slower iteration speed due to yield overhead.

useCases: Summing fields from massive CSV files
Inline argument pipelines for sum(), any(), all()
